configKeys.description: Directory used for Browser Control runtime state, including sessions, logs, screenshots, profiles, and durable stores. Set this when you need an isolated workspace or a non-default data location.
Port for the local broker HTTP API and WebSocket server. Change this when another service already uses the default port or when running multiple instances.
Chrome DevTools Protocol port used when attaching to a visible browser. The launcher scripts and attach-mode commands must use the same port.
Network address where launched Chrome exposes its debugging port. Keep this on localhost unless you explicitly understand the remote-control risk.
Explicit filesystem path to the Chrome or Chromium executable. Use this when automatic browser discovery picks the wrong binary or cannot find Chrome.
Full Chrome DevTools Protocol endpoint to attach to instead of deriving one from host and port. Use this for remote CDP providers, forwarded ports, or non-standard browser endpoints.
Controls whether Browser Control attaches to an existing browser or launches a managed browser it owns. Use `attach` for a visible user browser and `managed` for isolated automation sessions.
Allows Browser Control to launch an attachable browser when attach mode cannot find one. Disable this when browser startup must be manual or externally supervised.
Profile mode used by the visible browser launcher. Use `isolated` for a Browser Control profile and `system` only when you intentionally want the normal Chrome profile.
Explicit Chrome user-data directory for the visible launcher. Use this to point launched Chrome at a known profile directory without relying on the default profile manager.
Default viewport width for automation-owned browser contexts. This affects screenshots, layout-sensitive interactions, and tests that depend on responsive breakpoints.
Default viewport height for automation-owned browser contexts. This affects visible page area, screenshot dimensions, and scroll behavior.
User-Agent string applied to automation-owned browser contexts. Use this only when a target site or test requires a specific client identity.
Default safety policy profile for sessions and actions. Use `safe` for stricter automation, `balanced` for normal work, and `trusted` only for high-trust local workflows.
Controls whether daemon helper processes use a visible console window on Windows. Keep it false for background operation and enable it only when debugging daemon startup.
Minimum severity level written to logs. Use `debug` for diagnosis and `info` or higher for normal local operation.
Controls whether logs are also persisted to files under the runtime data home. Enable this when you need post-run diagnostics beyond terminal output.
Default shell executable or named shell for terminal sessions. Use this to prefer PowerShell, bash, zsh, or another installed shell.
Default terminal width in columns for new terminal sessions. Increase this when commands wrap too aggressively or when testing wide terminal layouts.
Default terminal height in rows for new terminal sessions. Increase this when interactive tools need more visible screen space.
Policy for reconstructing terminal sessions after daemon startup. Use `resume` to restore metadata and buffer, `metadata_only` to preserve identity only, or `abandon` to start clean.
Controls whether terminal sessions are automatically restored when the daemon starts. Disable this when stale terminal state is more harmful than losing prior session context.
WebSocket or HTTPS endpoint for a Browserless remote browser provider. Configure this when browser sessions should run outside the local machine.
API key sent to the Browserless provider when the endpoint does not already include a token. This is sensitive and is redacted from config inventories.
CAPTCHA solving provider used by browser automation when solver support is enabled. Leave this unset unless a workflow explicitly needs CAPTCHA solving.
API key for the configured CAPTCHA solving provider. This is sensitive and should only be set for workflows that are allowed to use solver services.
Canonical model provider used by the Browser Control model router. Choose `openrouter`, `ollama`, or `openai-compatible` based on where model requests should be sent.
Canonical base URL override for the selected model provider. When modelProvider is `openrouter`, this takes precedence over legacy openrouterBaseUrl.
Canonical API key for the selected model provider in the model router. When modelProvider is `openrouter`, this sensitive value takes precedence over legacy openrouterApiKey.
Canonical model identifier used by the selected model provider. When modelProvider is `openrouter`, this takes precedence over legacy openrouterModel.
Legacy OpenRouter model slug kept for older AI-agent configuration. Prefer modelName because canonical model* settings take precedence when both are configured.
Legacy OpenRouter base URL kept for compatibility with older configuration. Prefer modelEndpoint because canonical model* settings take precedence when both are configured.
Legacy API key for OpenRouter-backed AI-agent features. Prefer modelApiKey because canonical model* settings take precedence when both are configured.
